classifier_quote: die Auswahl und Einführung eines neuen ERP-Systems
classifier_reason: The company is the end user and is explicitly planning an ERP system selection and implementation as a major transformation project.

evidence.quote: die Auswahl und Einführung eines neuen ERP-Systems

brief.signal_summary: AES Aircraft Elektro/Elektronik System GmbH is hiring an ERP project lead for a strategically important transformation program to select and implement a new ERP system over the coming years.
brief.why_now: When companies start ERP selection and implementation, decisions about what stays active in the new core versus what is retained in an archive often get shaped very early by process design, data migration scope, and sizing assumptions. In ERP renewal programs, that matters because carrying too much historical data and document volume into the new environment can increase implementation effort and future run cost, while leaving legacy access unresolved can slow down decommissioning later.
brief.signal_quote: Für ein strategisch bedeutendes Zukunftsprojekt suchen wir einen ERP-Projektleiter (m/w/d), der die Auswahl und Einführung eines neuen ERP-Systems in den kommenden Jahren verantwortet.
stakeholder_hypothesis: Head of ERP Applications / ERP Competence Lead
Head of Operations or Manufacturing IT

angles.title: ERP selection phase creates a narrow window to define historical data scope
angles.detail: The posting explicitly combines ERP selection and implementation responsibility in one role. That is the point where active-data scope, historical document access, and any future legacy-system shutdown path can be designed into the target architecture instead of patched in later.

angles.title: Aerospace electronics context raises the importance of long-lived quality and traceability records
angles.detail: For aerospace electronics manufacturers, historical production, quality, and traceability-related records can have long retention and lookup needs. In a new ERP introduction, the practical split between what must remain operationally live and what can sit in compliant archive access is usually more sensitive than in less regulated environments.

angles.title: Multi-year transformation increases the risk of archive decisions being deferred
angles.detail: The posting says the new ERP system will be selected and introduced 'in den kommenden Jahren'. In longer ERP programs, teams often move core process and implementation topics first, while legacy documents and old transactional history remain unresolved until migration scope and operating model are already harder to change.

discovery_questions: Which role in the ERP program will own the decision on historical ERP data and legacy document access?
Is the target concept already separating operational data needed in the new ERP from older records that only need compliant access?
For AES's manufacturing and quality processes, which historical records are truly needed day-to-day versus only for audit, traceability, or occasional lookup?
Is the team planning for legacy system shutdown as part of the program, or only for the new ERP go-live?
Has data scope for the future ERP already been discussed in the context of implementation effort, storage footprint, or migration complexity?

email_slots.opener_de: bei der Auswahl und Einführung eines neuen ERP-Systems legt das Zielbild für Fertigungs-, Qualitäts- und Altbelege den Datenumfang der Einführung bereits mit fest.

flags: Evidence shows an ERP selection and implementation program, but does not confirm SAP or S/4HANA specifically.
The posting gives no concrete timeline beyond 'in den kommenden Jahren'.
Only one role is visible in the evidence; archive and legacy-data decisions may still be made jointly by IT, finance, operations, and quality/compliance.

rendered_email.subject: Datenaltlast vor dem S/4-Cutover bei AES
rendered_email.body:
Hallo,

bei der Auswahl und Einführung eines neuen ERP-Systems legt das Zielbild für Fertigungs-, Qualitäts- und Altbelege den Datenumfang der Einführung bereits mit fest.

Bei Luftfahrtzulieferern liegen erfahrungsgemäß 40 bis 60 Prozent der ECC-Datenbestände außerhalb der laufenden Nutzung. Wandern sie ungefiltert ins S/4-Sizing, kostet das im HANA-Footprint schnell einen sechsstelligen Betrag pro Jahr.

Wir haben für AES eine kurze Potenzial-Einschätzung vorbereitet: welche Datenklassen sich vor der Migration archivieren oder stilllegen lassen und was das im Sizing typischerweise spart. Eine Seite, in zwei Minuten gelesen.

Soll ich sie Ihnen schicken? Ein kurzes Ja genügt.

Mit freundlichen Grüßen
[SDR Name]

evidence.quote: Du wirkst aktiv an der Einführung von SAP S/4HANA mit

brief.signal_summary: Hamburger Hochbahn AG is hiring subproject leads for both SAP S/4HANA FI and SAP S/4HANA MM, indicating an active, cross-functional S/4HANA implementation with internal ownership across finance and materials processes.
brief.why_now: Companies in an active S/4 migration typically have a narrow window before scope, sizing, and process design harden. That is the point where decisions on what stays active in S/4 versus what can move into compliant archive or a legacy retirement model have the biggest impact on HANA footprint, migration effort, and post-go-live complexity. When FI and MM are both being implemented in parallel, the handling of historical accounting, purchasing, and document-heavy transactional data often cuts across multiple workstreams and external implementation partners.

angles.title: MM subproject suggests document-heavy purchasing history is in scope discussions
angles.detail: The MM posting says the hire will lead Materials Management implementation and translate business requirements into SAP S/4HANA processes. That is a specific point to ask how legacy purchasing and materials documents will be separated between operational access and retained history.

angles.title: Cross-functional program increases risk of archive decisions falling between teams
angles.detail: Both postings describe a 'bereichsübergreifenden Team' / coordination with internal and external stakeholders. In that setup, ownership for old SAP data and documents often gets blurred between FI, MM, migration, and implementation partners.

angles.title: User-story driven design is a good hook for legacy-access requirements
angles.detail: The FI posting mentions creating and structuring 'klare User Stories' from business concepts. That gives a concrete way in: whether legacy document access, historical lookup, and audit retention are already being written into the S/4 design backlog or left outside the main scope.

discovery_questions: In the S/4HANA program, who owns the decision on what historical FI and MM data remains active versus archived?

Is the team expecting to keep any legacy SAP environment available after migration for finance or materials lookups?

evidence.quote: Im Rahmen unseres SAP S/4HANA Transformationsprojekts

brief.signal_summary: Kärcher is hiring a Senior SAP Platform Architect for its SAP Technology Team, with the role explicitly tied to an SAP S/4HANA transformation project, leadership of complex SAP subprojects, and standards for operating hybrid SAP landscapes.
brief.why_now: This is a timely signal because companies in an S/4HANA transformation typically have to decide how much legacy ECC data and document history should remain in the new core versus move into an archive or legacy-access model. That decision often affects HANA sizing, migration scope, and the later option to decommission old systems. When a company is simultaneously shaping SAP architecture standards, subprojects, and hybrid landscape operations, the legacy-data approach can become part of the platform design rather than a cleanup topic left for later.

That points to active design and sequencing work where the boundary between active S/4 data and retained legacy information may still be set.

angles.title: Best-practice orientation creates an opening for archive-by-design decisions

classifier_reason: The company is a utility end user and the posting explicitly mentions an SAP IS-U to S/4 Utilities transformation, which is a strong ERP transformation signal.

evidence.quote: Im Zuge der Transformation von SAP IS-U zu SAP S/4 Utilities

brief.signal_summary: enercity Netz is hiring a fixed-term "Fachgebietsleiter:in Messdatenmanagement & SAP Reporting" tied to an active SAP Utilities transformation, with explicit responsibility for keeping meter-data, billing/prognosis, and SAP reporting stable while moving from SAP IS-U to SAP S/4 Utilities.
brief.why_now: Companies moving from SAP ECC-based utility landscapes to S/4 typically have to decide early which historical operational data and documents stay in the productive scope versus move into archive access. That matters because legacy volume can affect HANA sizing, migration effort, and the ability to decommission old systems later. In utilities, this is often more sensitive because billing, forecast, and meter-data processes still need reliable historical access during and after the transition.
brief.signal_quote: Im Zuge der Transformation von SAP IS-U zu SAP S/4 Utilities begleitest du die Weiterentwicklung datengetriebener Prozesse bis hin zur Stärkung moderner Reporting- und Analyticsansätze, insbesondere im Umfeld von Power BI, Datasphere und SAC.
stakeholder_hypothesis: Head of SAP Utilities / SAP S/4 Utilities program lead
IT lead for billing and metering processes
Data management or information governance lead

angles.title: Meter-data history vs productive S/4 scope
angles.detail: The role owns Messdatenmanagement and must ensure stable meter-data processes for billing and forecasting during the IS-U to S/4 Utilities transformation. A useful angle is how much historical meter-related data needs to remain in the productive target system versus being served from archive-access without disrupting day-to-day operations.

angles.title: Reporting continuity across IS-U, S/4 Utilities, and analytics tools
angles.detail: The posting explicitly links the transformation to SAP-based reporting plus Power BI, Datasphere, and SAC. That creates a concrete discussion about whether historical IS-U reporting data will be replicated into new analytics layers, left in legacy, or exposed through archived access for audit and comparison use cases.

angles.title: Operational stability during a long-running transition
angles.detail: The hire is fixed-term until 31.12.2027 and is tasked with stable, timely, high-quality operation of billing/prognosis-related meter-data processes. That suggests a multi-year transition window where retaining access to old documents and legacy data without carrying the full operational footprint can be commercially and operationally relevant.

discovery_questions: For billing and forecast processes, which historical IS-U data must users still access day-to-day after the move to S/4 Utilities?
Who in the transformation owns the decision on productive data scope versus archive/legacy access for Messdatenmanagement and SAP reporting?
How are you planning to handle historical reporting continuity across SAP reporting, Power BI, Datasphere, and SAC during the transition?
Is there already a target for decommissioning legacy IS-U components after the S/4 Utilities move, or will they remain for historical lookup?
Which document and retention requirements are most sensitive in your regulated utility context when old system access is reduced?

rendered_email.subject: Datenaltlast vor dem S/4-Cutover bei Otto Group
rendered_email.body:
Hallo,

bei Ihrer Retail-Accounting-S/4HANA-Systemlandschaft: ist die Trennung zwischen aktiven Daten und Archiv bereits entschieden oder noch in Planung?

Bei handelsgruppen liegen erfahrungsgemäß 40 bis 60 Prozent der ECC-Datenbestände außerhalb der laufenden Nutzung. Wandern sie ungefiltert ins S/4-Sizing, kostet das im HANA-Footprint schnell einen sechsstelligen Betrag pro Jahr.

Wir haben für Otto Group eine kurze Potenzial-Einschätzung vorbereitet: welche Datenklassen sich vor der Migration archivieren oder stilllegen lassen und was das im Sizing typischerweise spart. Eine Seite, in zwei Minuten gelesen.

Soll ich sie Ihnen schicken? Ein kurzes Ja genügt.

Mit freundlichen Grüßen

brief.signal_summary: ZEISS is hiring for an "Internship in Global Supply Chain Business Transformation (f/m/x)," which is a strong indicator of an active, globally scoped supply chain transformation program tied to ERP/process change.
brief.why_now: Companies in S/4 and broader ERP transformation phases typically have a narrow window to decide what operational data must move into the new core versus what can be archived or left accessible outside it. In global supply chain programs, that decision affects HANA sizing, migration effort, and later legacy decommissioning. If legacy data and document scope are left implicit until design or sizing is underway, teams often carry more historical load into the target environment than they actually need day to day.

angles.detail: The posting names "Business Transformation," which can indicate broader target-process work. That is often the point where archive scope gets overlooked unless someone explicitly separates active ERP data from historical records needed for audit, service, or operational reference.

classifier_reason: Berlin TXL Management GmbH appears to be the operating company itself and is explicitly hiring for ERP system management and introduction.

evidence.quote: Das strategische und operative Management unseres ERP-Systems sowie weiterer IT-Fachanwendungen und Plattformen liegt in deinen Händen.

brief.signal_summary: Berlin TXL Management GmbH is hiring a "Projektmanager*in | Systemmanager*in ERP Einführung" to own strategic and operational management of the ERP system and the interaction between ERP, CRM, DMS, DATEV, and other specialist applications.
brief.why_now: When companies are introducing or reshaping ERP landscapes, the legacy-data model usually gets locked in indirectly through integration, document flows, and sizing assumptions. In that phase, teams typically need to decide what remains active in ERP, what belongs in DMS, and what should move into a compliant archive so old data does not expand the future operating footprint or keep legacy environments alive longer than necessary.
brief.signal_quote: Damit unsere Systemlandschaft reibungslos funktioniert, verantwortest du das Zusammenspiel von ERP, CRM, DMS, DATEV und weiteren Fachanwendungen.
stakeholder_hypothesis: Finance Systems or Accounting Process Owner

angles.title: ERP-CRM-DMS-DATEV handoffs create document-boundary decisions
angles.detail: The posting explicitly says the role will manage the interaction of "ERP, CRM, DMS, DATEV und weiteren Fachanwendungen." That is a concrete point where teams need a clear rule for which business documents stay operationally active versus which are archived compliantly outside the core ERP flow.

angles.title: DMS is already in scope, so archive responsibility may be fragmented
angles.detail: Because DMS is named alongside ERP and DATEV, Berlin TXL likely has multiple systems touching documents and records. The practical angle is not generic cost savings, but avoiding split ownership where ERP, DMS, and finance each assume another system will retain legacy documents.

angles.title: Microsoft-oriented ERP introduction can still inherit legacy-document load
angles.detail: The posting welcomes knowledge in "Microsoft Business Central und Microsoft Dynamics." In ERP introductions like this, one recurring issue is that old finance and operational documents are carried forward implicitly unless archive scope is defined early across ERP and adjacent applications.

discovery_questions: In the ERP introduction, who owns the target model for legacy data and old documents across ERP, DMS, and DATEV?
For documents that originate in ERP but are needed in CRM, DMS, or finance processes, which system is meant to become the long-term record?
Is the team separating operationally needed data from retention-only data before the future ERP scope is finalized?
Are there older specialist applications in the landscape whose data must remain accessible after the ERP rollout, or is everything expected to stay online in productive systems?

classifier_reason: An end-user company is hiring for ERP transformation and project management, indicating active ERP landscape work.

brief.signal_summary: Fliegl Agro-Center is hiring an ERP Project Manager focused on ERP system management, project delivery, and integration work, which indicates active ERP landscape change with rollout, update, and upgrade responsibility.
brief.why_now: Companies in active ERP transformation phases typically have to decide early how much legacy ERP data and historical documents remain in the productive target landscape versus move into an archive or legacy access model. That choice can affect sizing, migration effort, compliance handling, and later decommissioning of old systems. When a team is formalizing requirements, running rollouts, and integrating ERP with surrounding applications, the legacy-data split often becomes harder to change later.

angles.title: Rollout, updates, and upgrades create a timing point for legacy decisions
angles.detail: The posting explicitly names "Leitung von ERP-Projekten (Rollout-Planung, Updates, Upgrades)". In programs with rollout and upgrade scope, the cut between active ERP data and retained history is often decided around template finalization and sizing, not after go-live.

angles.title: Requirements and documentation suggest a formal design phase
angles.detail: The role includes "Erstellung von Konzepten, Lastenheften und Dokumentationen für neue ERP-Anforderungen". If Fliegl is documenting new ERP requirements now, archive access, retention, and legacy-document availability can be framed as design requirements rather than treated as a later technical cleanup.

angles.title: ERP integration with APIs and SQL databases can expose historical-data dependencies
angles.detail: The posting calls out "Integration des ERP-Systems mit weiteren IT-Anwendungen (APIs, SQL-Datenbanken)". That can be a useful entry point to ask which downstream applications still need historical ERP records or documents once the core ERP landscape changes.

discovery_questions: In your rollout and upgrade planning, when is the legacy-data decision settled: before sizing or only later in the program?
Which historical ERP data and documents does the business still need operationally day to day versus only for audit or lookup?
Are surrounding applications connected via APIs or SQL expected to keep reading historical ERP records after the transformation?
Is the ERP Project Manager role also shaping data retention and archive access requirements in the concepts and specifications?
Do you expect any older ERP-connected applications or databases to remain in place temporarily after the main ERP changes?
